topic: Operations, Finance and Influences on Business
subject: business-studies

items.q: Are profit and cash flow the same thing?
items.correct: No — profit is revenue minus costs over a period, while cash flow is the timing of money in and out; credit sales create profit before any cash arrives.
items.misconception: If a business is profitable its cash flow must be healthy.
items.random_wrong: Cash flow is the money kept in the office safe.
items.why: The profit-cash confusion is the most common error in GCSE finance answers.

items.q: Does quality assurance mean a business never produces faulty goods?
items.correct: No — it reduces faults by building checks into each stage, but errors can still occur; it lowers the chance and cost of defects rather than eliminating them.
items.random_wrong: Quality assurance is a certificate bought from the government.
items.why: Students overstate what QA achieves; examiners reward recognising that it manages rather than removes risk.

items.q: Is flow production always better than job production?
items.correct: No — flow gives low unit costs for standardised products, but job production suits bespoke, high-value goods; the best method depends on the product and its market.
items.misconception: Mass production methods are superior for every business.
items.random_wrong: Job production is only allowed for charities.
items.why: Students rank methods absolutely instead of matching them to product type and demand.

items.q: Flow production is most suitable for:
items.correct: High volumes of identical, standardised products
items.why: The answer examiners want is: High volumes of identical, standardised products. “One-off bespoke products” is the distractor this question was built around — if it tempted you, that's the exact gap to close.

items.correct: Selling price minus variable cost per unit
items.misconception: Selling price minus fixed cost per unit
items.why: It's Selling price minus variable cost per unit. “Selling price minus fixed cost per unit” is the classic near-miss here; knowing why it's wrong is worth as much as knowing the right answer.

items.q: Which source of finance does NOT have to be repaid?
items.why: Correct answer: Retained profit. “A bank loan” sounds right because it borrows the language of the topic — but it fails this question. Re-read the notes on why.

items.q: A business with a negative net cash flow in a month has:
items.correct: Cash outflows greater than cash inflows that month
items.misconception: Definitely made a loss for the year
items.why: It's Cash outflows greater than cash inflows that month. “Definitely made a loss for the year” is the classic near-miss here; knowing why it's wrong is worth as much as knowing the right answer.

items.q: Fixed costs are £12,000, selling price is £8 and variable cost is £5 per unit. What is break-even output in units?
items.why: Exact answer: 4000 units. Estimation gets you near it; only the anchored number survives the exam hall.
